intro.speaker: Cipher
lines: Come into the Backchannel, I want to show you something unsettling. Three PULSE users' feeds, side by side.
All three swear they're seeing 'the world'. But each one sees a completely different reality, because ALGO only feeds them what they already agree with.
That's an echo chamber, Agent. People hearing their own echo louder and louder. Let's expose it - and pop one bubble from the inside.

learningGoals.media: Die Schueler koennen beschreiben, wie Echokammern und Filterblasen entstehen, welchen Effekt sie auf die eigene Meinung haben (Bestaetigung statt Vielfalt) und wie man sie aktiv durchbricht (bewusst andere Quellen suchen).
learningGoals.english: Die Schueler koennen Standpunkte und Argumente in englischen Kurztexten vergleichen und Vokabular zu Meinung und Perspektive anwenden (opinion, perspective, to agree/disagree, one-sided, balanced, bias).

puzzles.prompt: You scroll through all three feeds. They all cover the exact same question - 'Should the town build a new skate park?' - yet they feel like three separate worlds. What is really going on?
options: The three users live in three different towns with different news.
Each feed shows only one side of the question, so every user thinks 'everyone agrees with me'.
The topic is genuinely too complicated to have more than one view.
ALGO simply forgot to update two of the feeds.

feedback.correct: Exactly. Same topic, but each feed is filtered down to a single side. The feeling that 'everyone agrees with me' is manufactured.
feedback.wrong: Look again - it's the same town and the same question in all three. The difference is that each feed has been filtered to show just one side.

hints: Look at all three feeds at once. They're about the same topic - so why do they feel like three different worlds?
Each feed only shows one side. Ask what's missing from each one.
Each feed shows only one side of the question, so every user is left thinking 'everyone agrees with me'.

puzzles.prompt: On the investigation board, sort these statements pulled from the three feeds into 'pro', 'contra' and 'neutral fact'. As you do, notice how lopsided the feeds really are.
items: 'A skate park would finally give teens somewhere safe to hang out!'
'Building it will just bring noise and litter to our quiet street.'
'The council has set aside a plot of land near the river for the proposal.'
'Every kid I know is desperate for this - it's a no-brainer!'
'Nobody asked the neighbours, and that's not fair on residents.'
'The estimated cost of the project is 80,000 pounds.'

feedback.correct: Well sorted. Now you can see it: one feed was nearly all 'pro', another nearly all 'contra', and the neutral facts were missing everywhere.
feedback.wrong: Re-check a few. Excited support goes to 'pro', complaints and worries to 'contra', and plain checkable statements (land, cost) to 'neutral fact'.

hints: Is the statement cheering the park on, arguing against it, or just stating a checkable fact with no opinion?
Watch for the neutral facts - the plain, checkable statements about land and cost that carry no opinion.
The two excited-support lines are 'pro', the two complaint lines are 'contra', and the land plot and the cost are 'neutral fact'.

puzzles.prompt: Feed A is almost entirely 'pro' skate park. To repair the bubble and make it balanced, select the missing pieces - the counter-arguments and neutral facts ALGO filtered out - that you should add back in.
options: 'Some neighbours are worried about noise late at night.'
'The project would cost the town 80,000 pounds.'
'Skate parks are the best thing ever, full stop!'
'Not everyone skates, so is it fair to spend the budget on it?'
'Anyone against the park just hates young people.'

feedback.correct: That's a balanced feed. You added the missing worries and the neutral cost fact - the perspectives ALGO had quietly stripped out.
feedback.wrong: Careful. Adding more one-sided cheering or an insult doesn't balance a feed. Add the genuine counter-points and the neutral facts that were missing.

hints: The feed is already stuffed with support. What's missing is the other side and the plain facts.
Avoid anything that's just more cheering, and avoid insults aimed at the other side - those don't balance a feed.
Add the noise worry, the cost fact and the fairness question. Leave out the extra cheering and the insult.

puzzles.prompt: In a private chat, a friend inside the bubble writes, convinced: 'The skate park is obviously perfect and anyone against it is an idiot.' You want to open a window on another perspective without starting a fight. Which reply is best?
options: 'You're completely wrong and you clearly haven't thought about it.'
'I get why you're keen on it. Have you seen the neighbours' side about the noise and cost, though?'
'Yeah, totally, anyone who disagrees is an idiot.'
'Whatever, I don't care about the skate park anyway.'

feedback.correct: Perfect. You acknowledged their view and then calmly brought in another perspective. That's how you pop a bubble without a fight.
feedback.wrong: That reply either attacks, agrees blindly or shuts down. The best move recognises their view first, then gently adds the missing perspective.

hints: You want to add a perspective, not win a fight. How do you keep the friend listening?
Attacking them or agreeing blindly both fail. Acknowledge their view first, then offer another angle.
The best reply says you understand their view, then calmly asks if they've seen the other side (noise and cost).

outro.speaker: Cipher
lines: That's it. You didn't win an argument - you opened a window. That's how you pop a bubble without a fight.
Comfortable feeds feel nice, but they're just mirrors. Keep looking for what ALGO cut out. Great work, Agent.
